name: Quiver of Eros
shortDescription: Eros's quiver holds arrows that incite love or aversion in those struck.
description: The Quiver of Eros is a powerful artifact in Greek mythology, belonging to the god of love and desire, Eros (Cupid in Roman mythology). It is depicted as a container filled with arrows, each possessing the magical ability to incite either intense love and passion or insurmountable aversion in those struck by them. The arrows are not uniform; some are golden with dove feathers, tipped with sharp points to induce love, while others are blunt, leaden, and tipped with owl feathers to incite hatred and indifference. Eros uses these arrows to manipulate the emotions of gods and mortals alike, playing a crucial role in many mythological narratives. The quiver itself is sometimes described as being made of gold or precious materials, reflecting the preciousness and power of love itself.
subtitle: A Vessel of Love and Loathing
origin: Forged by Hephaestus, the Greek god of blacksmiths and craftsmen, for Eros.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Quiver of Eros is closely associated with the god's own origins. Eros, often depicted as a winged youth, is the son of Aphrodite, the goddess of love and beauty. The quiver is an integral part of Eros's iconography, symbolizing his power over love and desire. The most famous myth involving the Quiver of Eros is the story of Cupid and Psyche. In this tale, Eros is ordered by his mother to make Psyche fall in love with a hideous monster, but upon seeing her beauty, he accidentally pricks himself with one of his own golden arrows and falls deeply in love with her. This incident sets in motion a series of trials and tribulations for Psyche, ultimately leading to her immortality and union with Eros. The quiver is also mentioned in other myths where Eros intervenes in the affairs of gods and mortals, using his arrows to influence their romantic destinies.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Quiver of Eros plays a significant role in shaping the narratives of Greek mythology, particularly those involving love, passion, and relationships. It acts as a tool for the gods to manipulate the emotions of both mortals and immortals, often with dramatic consequences. The quiver emphasizes the unpredictable and sometimes chaotic nature of love, demonstrating its power to both unite and divide. Furthermore, it highlights the idea that love is not always rational or controllable, but rather a force that can be inflicted upon individuals, changing the course of their lives. The Quiver of Eros, therefore, represents the potent and often disruptive influence of love in the world.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Quiver of Eros is rich in symbolism. The arrows represent the piercing nature of love, its ability to strike unexpectedly and deeply. The golden arrows symbolize passionate love and desire, while the leaden arrows represent indifference, hatred, and the rejection of love. The duality of the arrows underscores the complex and multifaceted nature of love itself, encompassing both positive and negative emotions. The quiver, as a container for these arrows, symbolizes the potential for both joy and sorrow that love holds. It can also be interpreted as a metaphor for the power of desire and its capacity to influence human behavior. Overall, the quiver represents the potent and often unpredictable force of love, its ability to shape destinies, and its capacity to inspire both ecstasy and despair.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Quiver of Eros continues to be a potent symbol in modern culture, particularly in art, literature, and popular media. The image of Cupid (Eros) armed with his bow and quiver of arrows is a ubiquitous symbol of love and romance, frequently used in Valentine's Day imagery and other romantic contexts. The idea of being 'struck by Cupid's arrow' is a common metaphor for falling in love suddenly and unexpectedly. The concept of love as a powerful and sometimes uncontrollable force, symbolized by the quiver, remains relevant in contemporary understandings of relationships and emotions. Furthermore, the duality of the arrows (love and aversion) continues to resonate with modern audiences, reflecting the complex and often contradictory nature of human relationships.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: A primary source for many Greek myths, depicting Eros and his arrows.

sources.author: Apuleius
sources.description: Features the tale of Cupid and Psyche, showcasing the powers of Eros's arrows.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Represents the preciousness and value of love.

associations.name: Dove
associations.significance: Associated with Aphrodite and symbolizes love and peace.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Kama's Bow and Arrows
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Kama, the Hindu god of love, uses a bow and arrows to incite desire.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Aengus's Arrows
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Aengus Óg, the Irish god of love, sometimes has arrows of love or inspiration associated with him.

companions.name: Aphrodite
companions.description: Goddess of love and beauty; Eros's mother.

companions.name: Psyche
companions.description: Mortal woman who eventually becomes Eros's wife.

worshipPractices.name: Erotic Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Some ancient Greek cults involved rituals aimed at invoking Eros's power and influence in matters of love and fertility.

festivals.name: Erotes
festivals.description: Festivals dedicated to Eros and other deities associated with love and desire.

cultural.modernLegacy: The image of Cupid with his bow and arrow is a popular symbol of Valentine's Day and romantic love. The concept of being 'struck by Cupid's arrow' remains a common metaphor for falling in love.
